title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Aisha Khan, who organises a community garden.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're meeting Aisha Khan, who organises a community garden in her town. Aisha, how did the garden begin? || Guest: The land was empty for years, and people used to leave rubbish there. A few neighbours wanted the council to build a car park, but others thought the area needed somewhere green. [20]I joined the first meeting because I wanted children in the flats nearby to have a safe place to grow plants[/20]. At that time, I didn't know much about gardening myself. || Interviewer: What was the first big job? || Guest: Cleaning the land. We found old bottles, broken chairs and even a bicycle frame. It looked terrible. [21]The hardest part was not the heavy work, but persuading people that the garden could really become attractive[/21]. Some neighbours laughed when they saw us digging in the rain. || Interviewer: Who uses the garden now? || Guest: Lots of different people. Some retired people grow vegetables, parents bring young children, and office workers come at lunchtime. [22]What I'm happiest about is that people who didn't speak to each other before now share tools and advice[/22]. The garden has made the street feel friendlier. || Interviewer: Do you grow food for local families? || Guest: We do, but not enough to feed everyone. Last year we gave away tomatoes, beans and herbs. [23]This year we are planting more fruit because it is easy for children to pick and eat straight away[/23]. They enjoy strawberries especially. If children like the food they grow, they become more interested in healthy eating. || Interviewer: What problems do you have? || Guest: Water is the main one. We collect rainwater, but hot weeks are difficult. Some people think vandals are the biggest problem, but we rarely have trouble. [24]We need a better system for watering the plants when volunteers are away[/24], especially in August. || Interviewer: What are you planning for the future? || Guest: We want to build a small shelter, so workshops can continue when the weather is bad. But first, [25]we're starting a Saturday morning club where teenagers can learn to grow vegetables and cook simple meals[/25]. I hope it gives them useful skills and a reason to feel proud of the area. || Interviewer: How do you teach people who have never gardened before? || Guest: We start with simple jobs, such as filling pots, watering seedlings and pulling out weeds. Nobody is made to feel silly for asking basic questions. Gardening can seem mysterious, but plants mainly need light, water and patience. When people see their first seeds grow, they usually want to learn more. || Interviewer: Has the garden changed you personally? || Guest: Yes. I used to think community projects needed experts, but I have learnt that ordinary people can do a lot if they meet regularly and share responsibility. The garden has also made me slower in a good way. You cannot hurry a plant, and that is a useful lesson. || Interviewer: What advice would you give to another street that wants a garden? || Guest: Start small. A few pots outside a building can be enough to begin. If neighbours see something growing, they are more likely to join in. It is also important to celebrate small successes, like the first flower or the first meal cooked with vegetables from the garden.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're meeting Aisha Khan, who organises a community garden in her town. Aisha, how did the garden begin?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: The land was empty for years, and people used to leave rubbish there. A few neighbours wanted the council to build a car park, but others thought the area needed somewhere green. I joined the first meeting because I wanted children in the flats nearby to have a safe place to grow plants. At that time, I didn't know much about gardening myself.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was the first big job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Cleaning the land. We found old bottles, broken chairs and even a bicycle frame. It looked terrible. The hardest part was not the heavy work, but persuading people that the garden could really become attractive. Some neighbours laughed when they saw us digging in the rain.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Lots of different people. Some retired people grow vegetables, parents bring young children, and office workers come at lunchtime. What I'm happiest about is that people who didn't speak to each other before now share tools and advice. The garden has made the street feel friendlier.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you grow food for local families?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We do, but not enough to feed everyone. Last year we gave away tomatoes, beans and herbs. This year we are planting more fruit because it is easy for children to pick and eat straight away. They enjoy strawberries especially. If children like the food they grow, they become more interested in healthy eating.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Water is the main one. We collect rainwater, but hot weeks are difficult. Some people think vandals are the biggest problem, but we rarely have trouble. We need a better system for watering the plants when volunteers are away, especially in August.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are you planning for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We want to build a small shelter, so workshops can continue when the weather is bad. But first, we're starting a Saturday morning club where teenagers can learn to grow vegetables and cook simple meals. I hope it gives them useful skills and a reason to feel proud of the area.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do you teach people who have never gardened before?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We start with simple jobs, such as filling pots, watering seedlings and pulling out weeds. Nobody is made to feel silly for asking basic questions. Gardening can seem mysterious, but plants mainly need light, water and patience. When people see their first seeds grow, they usually want to learn more.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the garden changed you personally?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes. I used to think community projects needed experts, but I have learnt that ordinary people can do a lot if they meet regularly and share responsibility. The garden has also made me slower in a good way. You cannot hurry a plant, and that is a useful lesson.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give to another street that wants a garden?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Start small. A few pots outside a building can be enough to begin. If neighbours see something growing, they are more likely to join in. It is also important to celebrate small successes, like the first flower or the first meal cooked with vegetables from the garden.

questions.question: Why did Aisha first get involved with the garden?

options.A: She already had a lot of gardening experience.
options.B: She hoped to stop a car park being built near her home.
options.C: She wanted a safe green space for local children.

questions.explanation: Aisha says she joined because she wanted nearby children to have a safe place to grow plants.

questions.question: What was hardest at the beginning?

options.B: Convincing people the project could work.

questions.explanation: Aisha says the hardest part was persuading people that the garden could become attractive.

questions.question: What pleases Aisha most about the garden now?

options.A: Local people are talking and helping each other.
options.B: Office workers use the garden every day.
options.C: Retired people are growing many vegetables.

questions.explanation: Aisha says she is happiest that people who did not speak before now share tools and advice.

questions.question: Why is the garden growing more fruit this year?

options.A: It can be sold to local families.
options.B: It needs less water than vegetables.
options.C: Children can pick and eat it easily.

questions.explanation: Aisha says they are planting more fruit because it is easy for children to pick and eat.

questions.question: What problem does the garden need to solve?

options.B: Watering plants when volunteers are absent.

questions.explanation: Aisha says they need a better watering system when volunteers are away.

questions.question: What will the garden offer next?

options.A: Cooking and gardening sessions for teenagers.
options.C: A shop selling fruit and vegetables.

questions.explanation: Aisha says they are starting a Saturday club where teenagers can grow vegetables and cook simple meals.
